title: Graph data on line plots (through 1/8 of a unit)
description: Graph data from charts and frequency tables on dot plots. Data include fractions in halves, fourths, and eighths.
topic_path: Graph data on line plots (through 1/8 of a unit)

assessment_items.question_content:
For $4$ days, you record the number of hours you sleep each night.  You round each time to the nearest $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of an hour.

Day | Sleep time (hours)
:-: | :-:
Day $1$ | $7\dfrac{1}{4}$
Day $2$ | $7\dfrac{3}{4}$
Day $3$ | $7\dfrac{3}{4}$
Day $4$ | $8\dfrac{1}{2}$

**Create a line plot that shows all of the measurements on the number line below.**   
*Click above tick marks to add dots; click on tick marks to remove dots.*

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
The number line has $4$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{4}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.



[[☃ image 1]]

There are repeats in the data. Let's summarize the information into a frequency table.

Sleep time (hours) | Frequency
:-: | :-:
$7\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $1$
$7\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $2$
$8\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $1$

The frequency tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $2$ dots above the value $7\dfrac{3}{4}$ hours.

Now we can plot each sleep time and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The lengths of $5$ adult killer whales are shown in the table below.  The lengths are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac{1}{8}$ of a yard.

Whale | Length (yards)
:-: | :-:
Maestro | $7\dfrac{3}{4}$
Willy | $8\dfrac{7}{8}$
Namu | $8\dfrac{1}{4}$
Tom | $7\dfrac{5}{8}$
Midge | $7\dfrac{3}{4}$

**Create a line plot that shows all of the measurements on the number line below.**  
*Click above tick marks to add dots; click on tick marks to remove dots.*

 [[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
The number line has $8$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{8}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.


[[☃ image 1]]

There are repeats in the data. Let's summarize the information into a frequency table.

Length (yards) | Frequency
:-: | :-:
$7\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $2$
$8\dfrac{7}{8}$ | $1$
$8\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $1$
$7\dfrac{5}{8}$ | $1$

The frequency tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $2$ dots above the value $7\dfrac{3}{4}$ yards.

Now we can plot each length and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Gino records how much milk his family drinks each week in February.  

Week | Milk (gal)
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $3\dfrac{3}{4}$
$2$ | $2\dfrac{1}{4}$
$3$ | $3\dfrac{3}{4}$
$4$ | $1\dfrac{1}{2}$

**Create a line plot that shows all of the measurements on the number line below.**   
*Click above tick marks to add dots; click on tick marks to remove dots.*

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
The number line has $4$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{4}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.


[[☃ image 1]]

There are repeats in the data. Let's summarize the information into a frequency table.

Milk (gal) | Frequency
:-: | :-:
$3\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $2$
$2\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $1$
$1\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $1$

The frequency tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $2$ dots above the value $3\dfrac{3}{4}$ gallons.

Now we can plot each volume and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The owner of a produce stand is selling bunches of grapes, priced by the pound.  The table below shows the weights of several bunches of grapes rounded to the nearest $\dfrac{1}{8}$ of a pound.

Bunch | Weight (lb)
:-: | :-:
Bunch $1$ | $\dfrac{5}{8}$
Bunch $2$ | $1\dfrac{7}{8}$
Bunch $3$ | $1\dfrac{5}{8}$
Bunch $4$ | $\dfrac{6}{8}$
Bunch $5$ | $\dfrac{6}{8}$

**Create a line plot that shows all of the measurements on the number line below.**   
*Click above tick marks to add dots; click on tick marks to remove dots.*

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
The number line has $8$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{8}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.


[[☃ image 1]]

There are repeats in the data. Let's summarize the information into a frequency table.

Weight (lb) | Frequency
:-: | :-:
$\dfrac{5}{8}$ | $1$
$1\dfrac{7}{8}$ | $1$
$1\dfrac{5}{8}$| $1$
$\dfrac{6}{8}$ | $2$

The frequency tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $2$ dots above the value $\dfrac{6}{8}$ pounds.

Now we can plot each weight and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The amount of rain that fell in Tokyo, Japan for $3$ consecutive days is shown in the table below.  The measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac{1}{8}$ of an inch.

Day | Rainfall (in)
:-: | :-:
Day $1$ | $1\dfrac{1}{8}$
Day $2$ | $1\dfrac{3}{8}$
Day $3$ | $1\dfrac{3}{8}$

**Create a line plot that shows all of the measurements on the number line below.**   
*Click above tick marks to add dots; click on tick marks to remove dots.*

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
The number line has $8$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{8}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.


[[☃ image 1]]

There are repeats in the data. Let's summarize the information into a frequency table.

Rainfall (in) | Frequency
:-: | :-:
$1\dfrac{1}{8}$ | $1$
$1\dfrac{3}{8}$ | $2$

The frequency tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $2$ dots above the value $1\dfrac{3}{8}$ inches.

Now we can plot each measurement and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A family picked apples at an orchard. The table below shows how much each person picked, to the nearest $\dfrac{1}{4}$ bushel.

Person | Volume (bushels)
:-: | :-:
Saul | $1\dfrac{1}{4}$
Adrian | $1\dfrac{3}{4}$
Boden | $\dfrac{3}{4}$
Lize | $\dfrac{3}{4}$
Nyla | $1\dfrac{3}{4}$

**Create a line plot that shows all of the measurements on the number line below.**   
*Click above tick marks to add dots; click on tick marks to remove dots.*

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
The number line has $4$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{4}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.



[[☃ image 1]]

There are repeats in the data. Let's summarize the information into a frequency table.

Volume (bushels) | Frequency
:-: | :-:
$1\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $1$
$1\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $2$
$\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $2$

The frequency tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $2$ dots above the value $1\dfrac{3}{4}$ bushels.

Now we can plot each measurement and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:

:-: | :-:

$4\dfrac{1}{8}$ | $3$
$4\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $4$
$4\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $4$

hints:
The number line has $8$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{8}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.



[[☃ image 1]]

The number of baskets tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $2$ dots above the value $4\dfrac{3}{8}$ pounds.

Now we can plot each measurement and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Amy ran many miles during September. She recorded how long it took her to run each mile, rounded to the nearest $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of a minute, on the table below.

Time (min) | Frequency
:-: | :-:
$8\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $3$
$9\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $2$
$9\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $4$
$8\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $1$
$9$ | $5$


**Create a line plot that shows all of the measurements on the number line below.**   
*Click above tick marks to add dots; click on tick marks to remove dots.*

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
The number line has $4$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{4}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.



[[☃ image 1]]

The frequency tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $3$ dots above the value $8\dfrac{3}{4}$ minutes.

Now we can plot each measurement and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below  gives the heights of the saplings that Ms. Valendra’s science class planted.  The measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of an inch.

$8\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $5$
$7\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $4$
$9$ | $1$
$7\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $2$

*Click above tick marks to add dots; click on tick marks to remove dots.*

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
The number line has $4$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{4}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.


[[☃ image 1]]

The number of saplings tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $2$ dots above the value $8\dfrac{1}{4}\,\text{inch}$.

Now we can plot each measurement and its frequency.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the volumes of baking soda from several trials of a science experiment. The measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac{1}{8}$ teaspoon.

Volume (teaspoons) | Number of trials
:-: | :-:
$2\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $3$
$3\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $4$
$3\dfrac{1}{8}$ | $2$
$2\dfrac{5}{8}$ | $2$
$3\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $4$
$3\frac{3}{4}$ | $1$

**Create a line plot that shows all of the measurements on the number line below.**   
*Click above tick marks to add dots; click on tick marks to remove dots.*

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
The number line has $8$ equal parts per whole, so each interval has a length of $\dfrac{1}{8}$. 

Let's number the rest of the number line.


[[☃ image 1]]

The number of trials tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $3$ dots above the value $2\dfrac{1}{2}$ teaspoons.

Now we can plot each measurement and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the volumes of shampoo Nyla used for several days. The measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac{1}{8}$ ounce.

Volume (ounces) | Number of days

$\dfrac{3}{8}$ | $4$
$\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $2$
$\dfrac{5}{8}$ | $4$
$\dfrac{3}{4}$ | $1$

hints:

[[☃ image 1]]

The number of days tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $5$ dots above the value $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of an ounce.

Now we can plot each measurement and its frequency.

The correctly graphed measurements are:  

[[☃ plotter 2]]

$1$ | $5$
$2\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $1$

[[☃ image 1]]

The number of students tells us how many dots to put above each number on the line plot.  For example, we need $6$ dots above the value $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a mile.

Now we can plot each measurement and its frequency.
